one_line_takeaway: A Dagstuhl working-group report that frames LLM security around the data/model supply chain and a training-time (permanent) vs inference-time (ephemeral) attack split, arguing that LLMs' defining complexity is the root of largely still-hypothetical but well-founded vulnerabilities -- with attacks far ahead of defenses.
key_finding: The dominant theme is that LLM insecurity is driven by complexity that violates Saltzer and Schroeder's 'economy of mechanism': complexity in architecture, training data, training process, supply chain, deployment, and free-form user input each opens attack avenues, and several of them create hard or unavoidable trade-offs (e.g., a model that must 'interpret' user input is inherently exposed to injection/evasion; a rich data/model supply chain is inherently hard to keep uncompromised; a multi-task model is inherently exposed to backdoors, since a backdoor is essentially just another task). Key qualitative observations: web-scale public pre-training data is easily poisoned but the poisoned fraction is tiny, so universal damage is hard while *targeted* context-specific poisoning is feasible; fine-tuning data is smaller/higher-quality but a distinct poisoning vector; the model supply chain (provenance of the base model, fine-tuning stages, providers, human-feedback and RLHF pipelines) is a major and under-transparent risk surface; and community effort so far skews heavily toward demonstrating attacks one-by-one while mitigation work is scarce and some problems (e.g., backdoor detection) are intrinsically hard.
core_contribution: This is a working-group position/report paper (originating from Dagstuhl Seminar 23431 on 'Network Attack Detection and Defense - AI-Powered Threats and Responses') that structures the security analysis of large language models around four questions: (1) how LLM adversarial vulnerabilities differ from those of traditional ML, (2) what attack objectives apply to LLMs, (3) why security risk assessment for LLMs is hard, and (4) what the LLM data/model supply chain looks like and its security implications. Its own conceptual contribution is (a) a data supply-chain framing (Figure 1) that enumerates components -- pre-trained LLM, fine-tuned LLM, training data, human feedback, fine-tuning data, user feedback, external/RAG data -- and maps candidate vulnerability spots onto them; (b) a two-way classification of attacks by their effect on the model: training-time attacks causing permanent model poisoning versus testing/inference-time attacks causing only ephemeral, per-session effects; and (c) a catalog of open challenges organized into attacking LLMs, defending LLMs, and assessing attack impact. It explicitly scopes itself to LLMs as *targets* of adversarial attack, deliberately excluding the use of LLMs to generate malware or accelerate offensive operations (which it treats as an accelerator, not a new capability).
threat_model: Adversary aims to compromise an LLM or its outputs rather than to misuse an LLM as an offensive tool. The paper enumerates attacker goals: stealing the (expensive-to-train) model; denial of service (degrading responsiveness, possibly via inputs that make the model fail); privacy attacks that coax disclosure of sensitive data memorized from training; inducing systematic bias across all queries; model degeneration/degradation over time via prolonged interaction and feedback loops; and falsified/attacker-controlled output for specific queries (noted as not yet convincingly demonstrated for full output control). Attacker capabilities are tied to supply-chain access: contributing poisoned samples to web-scraped pre-training data or to curated fine-tuning data (permanent backdoors triggered by deliberate or even naturally-occurring input features such as sentiment, phrasing, or code patterns); poisoning human-annotator feedback or RLHF reward signals; poisoning user-conversation feedback that is folded back into model updates; prompt-injection/prompting attacks to evade alignment defenses within a session; algorithm-level attacks against transformer components (positional encoding, multi-head attention); man-in-the-middle adversaries interposing between user and model; and manipulation of derivative products / RAG external-data integration. Knowledge assumptions span white-, gray-, and black-box, aggravated by vendor opacity (e.g., GPT-4 withholding architecture/data/training details).
limitations: By the authors' own framing this is a discussion report, not an empirical study: many of the enumerated vulnerabilities are acknowledged as hypothetical or not yet demonstrated (e.g., complete adversarial control of outputs 'has yet to be demonstrated'). There is no experimental validation, no quantification of attack difficulty/effort (indeed 'how long / how much poisoned data' is posed as an open question), no proposed or evaluated defenses, and no metrics. The scope deliberately excludes offensive use of LLMs. The supply-chain taxonomy is coarse-grained by design. Conclusions are necessarily provisional given the rapid pace of LLM development the paper itself flags.
